speaker: interviewer
text: Here is Mr. Joseph Greenblatt, tape five. When did you go on that special mission to East Berlin on this travel? When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in 1946. In March of 1946. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you got to East Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got to East Berlin with my secretary, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was her false papers. And from over there, over there I left my military Jeep, changed my-- myself to a normal uniform, to a civilian suit, which was in my suitcase. And we smuggled ourselves out from East Berlin to West Berlin, and from West Berlin into West-- West Germany. I mean, to East-- from East Germany to West Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then from-- from this-- from Hanover, in-- in the British zone, we went to Munich, the American zone, where I met my friends from Betar. And I-- this-- had contact from-- with the Jewish brigade, especially with the [NON-ENGLISH]. And he made contact with my sister. Because there was no mail going with my sister in Belgium. Then I was smuggled into Belgium. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I continued my work in Belgium for the Irgun. I was officially the correspondent for the Irgun paper, which was published in Paris, [NON-ENGLISH] was the editor. That was my official stay in Belgium as a correspondent and a newspaper man. I was arrested in Belgium. I don't-- no, you were too young. You weren't in project even. But [NON-ENGLISH] was arrested in Paris for sending this mail with this explosives to members of the British-- represent the British. And of course, I was arrested. Although she was a member of the Stalin group, I was arrested in Belgium. That's why I have--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll bring it later.

speaker: interviewee
text: The medal for being arrested. The medal for being-- [BACKROUND NOISE] they call it [NON-ENGLISH]. The medal for sitting. [LAUGHS] And I continued my work till-- prepared buying these arms in Belgium, which was loaded on the ship, and I was constantly smuggling the arms in Belgium till 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: The foundation of the state.

speaker: interviewee
text: Foundation of Israel. Then my illegal activities ceased. I became a normal human beings.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a child in Belgium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter, Janine, or Miriam [NON-ENGLISH], named after her two grandmothers. She married in-- [BACKROUND NOISE] graduated City College here. And she married 1977. And I'll give you-- had one son here in New York. He lived here in the same building, after he married. He lived on the 20th floor, and I'm on the 17th-- who is named after my father and after his other grandfather. That's why it's Eric Scott Levy, [NON-ENGLISH] Levy. And my granddaughter is married-- is named after my wife's sister, sister which I said was my beloved, this, Renee, and after, my wife's grandmother, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you decided to come to America, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I decided to. My wife was afraid. She said, I have enough underground. I know the first thing, he'll come to Israel. You'll be in the Irgun. And I have enough of underground life. I was sleeping enough nights on mattresses filled [BACKROUND NOISE] with grenades and handguns. That was our-- our honeymoon, was on a mattress filled with guns and these hand grenades. Delaborated, of course. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So my wife-- my life was not a honeymoon. That is, I would say-- When did you come here?

speaker: interviewee
text: December 20, 1950. This is, like I would say, the moral part of my life. The physical part, which I can describe the rest of it is down deep in my heart. It'll follow me forever.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for a living when you came to America? I mean, you-- did you bring money? Did you have money?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. And I didn't get anything from the Joint. They told me, you have family. So my family, my aunt, my mother's sister, prepared for me an apartment on Clinton Street, in the Lower East Side, 45 Clinton Street. Where I lived for a short short while. Then my wife's aunt, my mother in law's sister, younger sister, got for me an apartment uptown, 111th Street. [BACKROUND NOISE] Clinton Street was a fifth floor walk up, with facilities in the hallway. That's when my daughter learned to speak Yiddish. Don't understand Yiddish. And because there were only Jews living there at that time, we were-- two weeks ago, I took my wife a ride downtown. I said, let's go the Jewish Heritage and the Unites States. And we went to Clinton Street, and Delancey Street, Pitt Street, Orchard Street, East Houston, and a few other streets in the neighborhood, Division Street, just to show how it look- how it looked now and then-- then and now. Really, had-- we enjoyed the ride. Then I worked for my cousin in a factory of bedspreads, a cousin whom I knew. We were children together. He lived in our house for a while, when his mother and father were already in America. I worked for him. And it-- it wasn't work for me. I wasn't cut out to work on a sewing machine. I didn't know how to do it. But still, I worked. I gained-- the minimum wage was at that time $25 an hour. I was privileged. I got $30.

speaker: interviewer
text: $25? Not an hour.

speaker: interviewee
text: A week. And I was privileged, and I got $30. Although, my rent was $25 a month. But this didn't take long. I was looking for better things. I got a job as a shipping clerk. So I worked for a while as a shipping clerk. Then through my connections in the Zionist Revisionist Movement [NON-ENGLISH], I got a job as a production supervisor at Yardney Electrics. I was working there as a production supervisor. Also, because of [NON-ENGLISH]. And then I went into part time in the travel business. Then I got my own travel travel agency in Queens. And then it came a time when I had to a little play it slow. After all, I'm now 81 years old. Next-- next Tuesday. Next Monday. So I'm going in and out of business. I've worked for the travel agency as a part time agent. And I go whenever I feel to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: You drive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you told me you spend a lot of time in California?

speaker: interviewee
text: I go to California for every major Jewish holiday. All the-- whenever I find a special reason. Like I went-- go for Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, and Sukkot, and Simchat Torah over there. My daughter built a nice sukkah. I'm building it when I come. I'm putting it up. I bought them the sukkah. It's a portable sukkah. So the children are happy. We invite some people over there for Sukkot, and they eat in the sukkah. When I was young, I eat-- now I enjoy it. [LAUGHS] And then, I go to all this-- I spend-- then I go four days for Pesach there. And then I had two bar-- bar mitzvahs in [INAUDIBLE]. Of course, I had to be there. I enjoy my being with the family. I'm very close with them. I was very close with my daughter. In fact, I was closer with my daughter [LAUGHS] than my wife was with her. [INAUDIBLE] She always looked to me. Until she got her first child. Then she made a 360 degrees turn around. Now she is Mommy's daughter. That's how life-- life must go on. And that's my short story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow. Thank you. A very [INTERPOSING VOICES] incredible story almost.

speaker: interviewee
text: I speak occasionally--

speaker: interviewer
text: Miraculous.

speaker: interviewee
text: --for for many Jewish organizations, which you will see from the- from my awards. I didn't join any. Are you still taping?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't join any of those organizations, because most of the people who called us a [NON-ENGLISH] or something don't even know where Warsaw is on the map.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now there is more survivors than-- people, Warsaw ghetto uprising survivors, than there was Jews in the Warsaw ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're also writing your autobiography?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, I have it right here. The first chapters. And [INAUDIBLE] just took his copy, because I left my copy with my daughter, and I want another copy to make corrections and other things. So I took his copy and make copies if you want. But I'll show it to you. It's no--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we're going to-- we're going to ask your wife to join us. And then we have some pictures, and we'll show them. But I want to thank you very much for sharing your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Listen, it's all--

speaker: interviewer
text: And for allowing us into your home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. The pleasure was mine of having you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I make my feelings a little bit easier in my heart. I got it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Well, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some moments maybe were a little bit emotional for me, but

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: A good cry is good. This is my father. That picture was taken when he was very young. I didn't know him yet, in those days. It was before I was born. That is the days when he was in the Polish underground fighting for the Polish independence.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] Grunblatt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when do you think it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. It had to be sometime in 1913 or 1914.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. It could have been very well in Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who is on this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yena. My sister, Helena. My brother, Siegmund. And in the middle, it's little me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, [SIGHS] I probably was here about five years old, maybe. So I would say in the early '20s.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did this picture survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: By my aunt, who lived here. And she gave me back the picture. Same way I got my father's picture. And this was taken in our summer home, on the outskirts of Warsaw in [NON-ENGLISH]. This is my picture taken in 1938, during the conflict between Poland and Czechoslovakia. That was the time I was nominated as a second lieutenant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's on this picture, Mr. Greenblatt?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife and I right after the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Introduce the people in this picture, Mr. Greenblatt.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my daughter, Jennie. Or with her Hebrew name, [INTERPOSING VOICES] Miriam [NON-ENGLISH]. And my two-- [BACKROUND NOISE] now our two grandchildren, Eric and Renee. This is Eric, our grandson, at his bar mitzvah, holding the Torah. And this was in Temple Beth Emet in Anaheim, California. This is our granddaughter, Renee. That's her bat mitzvah, also in Anaheim, California.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is an award given to me by the Fraternal [BACKROUND NOISE] Order of the Police in 198-- 1992.

speaker: interviewer
text: In recognition of exceptional personal integrity and exemplary conduct. And it was the first time, it says, that this was given to a civilian. And what is this plaque?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is an award given to me by the Council of Jewish Organizations in Civil Service, presented to me in 1994, as a remembrance award for his heroic efforts as a partisan and fighter and courageous participation in the battle of the Warsaw ghetto. The award symbolizes our appreciation for his outstanding courage. Louis Weiser, president, October 30, 1994. The first medal is a medal of [SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH]. Celebrating the 20th anniversary of restoring the revolt.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's on the left.

speaker: interviewee
text: The other one is the [NON-ENGLISH]. That means the medal for sitting, for being arrested. Also from the Irgun.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were joined by Mrs. Irene Greenblatt, who married you in the Warsaw ghetto, right? 1943?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And has been your partner ever since. Herself a survivor, but not quite ready to give her own testimony. So we just wanted to thank you for letting us photograph you and for letting us into your home, and we wish you many more years of good health together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we want to thank you for taking your time, for doing this marvelous work for the future.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the future other generations know the history, that not-- that the revolt, the Warsaw ghetto, partisan-- participation of Jews was not a myth. It was a reality. They suffered, and they fought, and conquered the enemy. [BACKROUND NOISE] [SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.
